title: Why I focus on building funded accounts instead of chasing payouts
author: ReceptionUnlucky9455
selftext:
The way I look at prop trading is more like a mix of math, mindset and having some kind of plan. I feel like a lot of people get too emotional with prop accounts and most of it comes from misunderstanding what is actually at risk.

When you buy an eval, you are not really trading a big account. You are paying a small amount to access a larger drawdown. That drawdown is not your money. Your real risk is just what you paid. But when people lose a trade or even blow an account, they react like they lost the full account size. That’s where things start going wrong.

So instead of thinking you lost thousands, it’s more accurate to think you lost the cost of doing business. It sounds simple but it changes how you react. If you believe you lost a huge amount, you get frustrated, revenge trade, break rules. If you understand the real loss is controlled, you stay calmer.

Also I don’t really see prop trading as just trading one account. It feels more like managing a bunch of attempts. The eval fee is just the cost to try. The funded account is like something that can generate income. If it fails, it’s just one attempt that didn’t work, not the end of everything.

Another thing I see a lot is people buying evals every month like it’s some subscription. That usually leads to constant resetting and unnecessary pressure. A better way, at least for me, is to first focus on getting to the max funded accounts allowed by that prop firm. Usually its 3 (Earn2Trade), or 5 (tradeify) etc ... Once you reach that, you stop buying new evals and just trade those accounts. Only when one dies, then you replace it.

This means there can be months where you don’t spend anything and just focus on trading. That part is important because it removes that constant pressure of needing to “make back the fee”.

If you are serious about this as a long term side thing, then you kind of have to treat it like one. Not just random trading, but actually planning and checking how you are doing against that plan. The first goal shouldn’t be payout. It should be reaching max funded accounts. Once you have multiple accounts, the pressure is not all on one. It spreads out.

In my case, I try to keep a pool of funded accounts running. There is some monthly cost but it’s not fixed, it mostly comes from replacing accounts when needed. When you have many accounts working at the same time, you don’t need all of them to win. Even a few doing well can make the whole thing work.

So overall it’s less about one trade or one account and more about keeping the system going. You are basically managing a process, not chasing a single win.

I think the biggest mistake is people attaching their emotions to the account size instead of the actual money they are risking. Once you shift that, things become a bit easier mentally.

At the end of the day, it’s just practice. You show up, you execute, some things work, some don’t. If you can stay in the game long enough without blowing yourself up mentally or financially, then you at least give yourself a real chance.

Also, starting to learn prop trading using a simple automated workflow can really help. It improves your productivity and takes a lot of the stress out, especially in the early stages.

comments.author: Alfadiverscozumel
comments.body:
Can you give an example
Of a simple automated workflow across let’s say 5 accounts

comments.author: ReceptionUnlucky9455
comments.body:
Tried to be simple here, but based on each prop firm things get complicated with payout cycle, consistency rules, account limits rth/eth, event trading allowed etc.. 

But to keep it simple- if I have 5 accounts, check each account conditions, apply my trade profile/s to each (Profit and risk, TSL…) I Never ever copy trade. 

So, once i have everything ready, I decide 5 times today to analyze the market like every top of the hour or so. Then, login to ninja at those times. Check the market for 10-15 mins and Wait for my ema conditions , enter the trade , leave it alone. After all trades are done , flatten all and close it. AMA
